text: No, they wouldn't need to turn around or burn extra fuel to get back. Artemis 2 flies a free-return trajectory: after the trans-lunar injection burn from Earth orbit, the Orion spacecraft is on a path where Earth's and Moon's gravity naturally curve it around the Moon (or past it) and slingshot it home—no further propulsion required. It's the same safety feature that saved Apollo 13. The European Service Module has plenty of delta-v for mid-course tweaks if needed, but the mission is designed to return safely even without them.

text: Let's make this a slightly longer post as it's important. Here's why today's successful conclusion of the Artemis 2 lunar mission is such a profound accomplishment. Up until a decade ago, the moon was a useless space rock. Until Indian and Japanese probes found ice. And NASA confirmed massive deposits of it later with ground penetrating orbital radar. Now, all of a sudden you have water, air and rocket fuel along with abundance of every single mineral imaginable. Whomever controls the moon by being first to stay & control the resources, controls the entire future of humanity in space. It's a stepping stone to build and launch massive space ships and space stations. Which you can't do from earth. It's bigger than the industrial revolution. While Earth's gravitational acceleration is roughly 9.8 \{m/s}, the Moon's is only about 1.625 {m/s}, meaning objects weigh about six times less on the moon. That means you can launch things six times bigger with just simple rocket technology we have today. But the physics get even cooler. It takes only 1/100th (or something silly of that nature) of orbital velocity to launch things into space from lunar surface and depart the orbit. Think starwars battle cruisers launched from a mile long maglev ramp by accelerating it on rail using rocket engines. United States red heart oncoming fist

text: I'm glad and definitely relieved that Artemis 2 voyagers made it safely back home and look forward to them being interviewed in the future and answering a few questions about their experiences. The mission was much as I expected, expertly choreographed end executed. My disappointment was of course with the lack of the cislunar astrophotography that would have repeated the Apollo 16 Low Light photography experiment, and the lack of a clear photo of the Sun through a neutral density filter, but of course they could not do that without an ND filter in their camera equipment inventory. Neither could the Apollo crews of course. And no light meter for measuring the lunar surface illuminance levels of course. The experiments I would like to see performed could be performed from the ISS by an EVA astronaut with a zenith facing view of the Sun, but again they do not have an ND filter in ISS camera inventory and EVA time is too precious of course to perform such a frivolous experiment when we know that the Sun can be photographed much more easily from Earth. Empirical scientific experiments be damned.
